summary: Gruber pushes back against the growing narrative that cheap Android phones spell doom for Apple, arguing that pundits are misreading the market dynamics. He contends that $100 Android phones threaten traditional handset makers like Nokia far more than Apple, which has always competed at the top of the market. He emphasizes that emotion is a central, not peripheral, factor in consumer purchasing decisions and that Apple's products are designed precisely around emotional appeal. He dismisses comparisons to Apple's 1990s decline against Windows, noting that Apple today holds the largest mobile software platform and mainstream emotional trust — advantages the Mac never had. The piece concludes by asking whether iOS can remain the leading platform without leading in unit sales, suggesting Apple and Google are playing fundamentally different games.
key_points: $100 Android phones threaten Nokia and traditional handset makers far more than Apple, which has always been positioned at the top of the market
The real cost comparison must include service contracts — a $99 iPhone with a $1,800 two-year contract isn't dramatically different from a free Android phone with the same plan
Emotion is a massive, perhaps dominant factor in consumer purchasing decisions, and dismissing Apple's emotional appeal means missing the entire point of their products
The 1995 Windows analogy fails because Apple today owns the largest mobile software platform and developer revenue — the opposite of its position in the PC era
iOS vs. Android may not be zero-sum: they are playing fundamentally different games, with Google profiting from ads and Apple from hardware and platform
money_quotes: Things only look disastrous for Apple if you count their ever-declining share of 'usable smartphones', rather than the company's share of phones, or even just smartphones.
The real race to expand smartphone sales is to reduce the cost of the monthly plans.
To discount the emotional appeal of Apple's products is to ignore the entire point of Apple's products.

There's an implied assumption in some of this handwringing that the iPhone (and iOS as a whole) only have a chance so long as they don't have any competition — that Apple is a delicate corporate flower that stands still and can't compete on price.
If Android handset makers can make a good phone for $100, couldn't Apple make a better one for the same price?
Apple today has the biggest mobile software platform, by far. That was never true for the Mac. But the biggest difference is that Apple, today, has an emotional trust with the mainstream mass market.
There's never been anything like Android before. But there's never been anything like iOS before either.
Can iOS remain the leading mobile platform without being the leading platform measured by device unit sales?
key_insight: Cheap Android phones threaten traditional handset makers, not Apple, because Apple competes on emotional appeal and platform value rather than price — and unlike the 1990s Mac, Apple today owns the dominant mobile software ecosystem.
